grammarLessons.name: 越来越 & 越...越... — More and More

cards.grammarNote:
Adverb form: **快乐地** (kuàilè de — happily).
In Chinese, add **地** (de) after an adjective to form an adverb: 她快乐地笑着。(She was smiling happily.)

Adverb form: **悲伤地** (bēishāng de — sadly).
Add **地** after the adjective: 他悲伤地点头。(He nodded sadly.)

**爱** (ài) functions as both a noun ('love') and a verb ('to love'). As a verb it takes an object directly: **我爱你** (I love you), **我爱我的家人** (I love my family).
**爱** vs **喜欢** (xǐhuān): 爱 is stronger and more emotionally deep; 喜欢 means 'like/enjoy.' You can 喜欢 food or hobbies, but 爱 implies deep affection.
The character **爱** contains the element **心** (heart) — literally carrying the heart inside.

**愤怒** (fènnù) = anger (noun or stative verb). **愤** = indignation/resentment, **怒** = rage/fury.
Both characters carry the heart radical **心** at the bottom — a common pattern for emotion characters.
**愤怒** is more intense and literary than **生气** (shēngqì), which is the everyday word for being angry.
Used as a verb: **他很愤怒** (He is very angry). As a noun in this card's phrase: **他的愤怒** (his anger).
**让** (ràng) + person + verb = 'make someone do something': **让大家害怕** = made everyone afraid.
cards.phrase: 他的愤怒让大家都害怕。
cards.phraseRomanized: tā de fènnù ràng dàjiā dōu hàipà.

cards.grammarNote:
**恐惧** (kǒngjù) = fear (noun). **恐** = dread/terror, **惧** = to fear.
Both characters contain the heart radical **心** — the left component **忄** (vertical heart radical, 竖心旁) in **惧**.
**恐惧** is more literary/formal than the everyday **害怕** (hàipà = to be afraid of).
**无法** (wúfǎ) = unable to / have no way to: **无法隐藏** = unable to hide. Compare **不能** (bù néng = can't).
cards.phraseRomanized: tā wúfǎ yǐncáng tā de kǒngjù.

cards.grammarNote:
**希望** (xīwàng) works as both a noun ('hope') and a verb ('to hope'). As a verb it is followed by a clause: **我希望你早日好起来** = I hope you get better soon.
**希** = to hope/wish, **望** = to gaze/expect. Together they express hopeful anticipation.
**早日** (zǎorì) = soon / at an early date (literally 'early day'). A polite, caring expression.
**好起来** = 'get better / recover' — **起来** (qǐlái) as a directional complement often signals improvement or an upward change of state.
cards.englishPhrase: I hope you feel better soon.
cards.phraseRomanized: wǒ xīwàng nǐ zǎorì hǎo qǐlái.

cards.grammarNote:
**压力** (yālì) = pressure / stress. **压** = to press/suppress, **力** = strength/force.
The phrase structure **[topic] + [verb phrase] + 很大** is a common Chinese pattern: **工作压力很大** = 'work stress is very big' (i.e., a lot of work stress).
Note: Chinese often uses **大** (big) and **小** (small) for intensity: **压力大** (a lot of stress), **压力小** (little stress), rather than 'much/little'.
Related word: **有压力** (yǒu yālì) = to have/feel stress.
cards.englishPhrase: He has a lot of stress at work.

cards.grammarNote:
**心情** (xīnqíng) = mood / state of mind. **心** = heart, **情** = emotion/feeling.
**情** contains the heart radical **忄** (竖心旁) on the left — a very common component in emotion words: **情感** (emotion), **激情** (passion), **友情** (friendship).
Pattern: **心情 + 很 + adjective** = 'mood is very [adj]': **心情很好** (in a good mood), **心情很差** (in a bad mood), **心情很复杂** (in a complicated mood).
Note: Chinese does NOT say ~~我是好心情~~ — use **我心情很好**, treating 心情 as the topic.
cards.englishPhrase: I'm in a good mood today.

cards.grammarNote:
**感觉** (gǎnjué) = feeling / sensation, or 'to feel / to sense'. Works as both noun and verb.
**感** = to sense/feel (also contains **心** heart at the bottom), **觉** = to perceive/awaken.
As a verb: **我感觉很好** (I feel great). As a noun: **一种奇怪的感觉** (a strange feeling).
Related words: **感受** (gǎnshòu) = feeling/experience (more about emotional experience), **感到** (gǎndào) = to feel (+ result, more formal).
Measure word **种** (zhǒng) is used for types/kinds: **一种感觉** = 'a kind of feeling.'
cards.phraseRomanized: wǒ yǒu yī zhǒng qíguài de gǎnjué.

cards.grammarNote:
**情感** (qínggǎn) = emotion / feeling (often used for deeper, more complex feelings).
**情** = emotion/feeling, contains **忄** (heart radical 竖心旁) on the left. **感** = to feel/sense, contains **心** (heart) at the bottom.
Both characters carry a heart element — a vivid reminder that emotions are 'of the heart' in Chinese.
Related: **感情** (gǎnqíng) = feelings/affection (especially between people), **情绪** (qíngxù) = emotions/mood (more psychological tone).
**激发** (jīfā) = to stir up / inspire / arouse.
cards.phrase: 音乐能激发深深的情感。
cards.phraseRomanized: yīnyuè néng jīfā shēnshēn de qínggǎn.

cards.grammarNote:
**兴奋** (xīngfèn) = excited / excitement. Functions as both adjective and noun in Chinese.
**兴** = spirit/interest/enthusiasm, **奋** = to exert/strive with energy.
**感染** (gǎnrǎn) = to infect / be contagious — can be used for emotions spreading, not just illness: **他的兴奋感染了所有人** = His excitement infected everyone.
The **了** after **感染** marks the completed event (everyone was affected).
Near-synonym: **激动** (jīdòng) = thrilled/moved (often with a stronger emotional surge), **高兴** (gāoxìng) = happy/pleased.
cards.phrase: 他的兴奋感染了所有人。
cards.phraseRomanized: tā de xīngfèn gǎnrǎn le suǒyǒu rén.

cards.grammarNote:
Adverb form: **平静地** (píngjìng de — calmly).
Add **地** after the adjective: 她平静地回答。(She answered calmly.)
cards.englishPhrase: She stayed calm in the chaos.
cards.phrase: 她在混乱中保持了平静。
cards.phraseRomanized: tā zài hùnluàn zhōng bǎochí le píngjìng.

cards.grammarNote:
**和平** (hépíng) = peace (especially world peace / absence of war). **和** = harmony/together, **平** = even/calm/flat.
For inner peace, use **内心平静** (nèixīn píngjìng) or **平和** (pínghé = calm and peaceful, of a person's temperament).
**渴望** (kěwàng) = to long for / yearn for. **渴** = thirsty (showing the intensity of the desire), **望** = to gaze/expect. Together: 'thirsting for.'
**都** (dōu) = all/both, requires a plural subject: **我们都** = we all.

**担心** (dānxīn) = to worry / be worried. **担** = to carry on one's shoulders / bear a burden, **心** = heart.
Literal meaning: to carry worry in the heart → to worry.
**心** appears directly in this word — one of the most transparent heart-character combinations.
**别** (bié) = don't (imperative, softer than **不要**): **别担心** = Don't worry.
**一切都好** = everything is fine. **一切** (yīqiè) = everything/all, always paired with **都** (all): **一切都好**, **一切都会好的**.

**惊讶** (jīngyà) = surprised / astonished. **惊** = to startle/be startled, **讶** = to be amazed.
**惊** contains the heart radical **忄** (竖心旁) on the left — the emotion of being startled lives in the heart.
Near-synonyms: **吃惊** (chī jīng) = to be shocked/startled (literally 'eat a fright' — very colloquial), **惊喜** (jīngxǐ) = pleasant surprise.
**满心** (mǎn xīn) = 'full heart / with all one's heart': **满心惊讶** = heart full of surprise. This is a vivid, slightly literary expression.

**激情** (jīqíng) = passion / intense enthusiasm. **激** = to excite/stir up (water radical 氵), **情** = emotion (heart radical **忄**).
The **情** character (emotion) contains the heart radical **忄** (竖心旁) — found in many emotion words: **激情**, **心情**, **情感**, **友情**.
**充满激情地** = passionately (literally 'filled with passion [manner]'). This uses **地** to form an adverb from a phrase: 充满激情地说话 = speak passionately.
Note: **地** (de) as adverb marker is pronounced the same as 的 but written differently and used only before verbs.

**渴望** (kěwàng) = to long for / desire / yearn. Works as both verb and noun.
**渴** = thirsty (water radical 氵 on left, suggesting physical craving), **望** = to gaze toward / hope for.
Together: 'to thirst for something' — a vivid image of intense desire.
As a verb, **渴望** directly precedes the object: **渴望旅行** = desire to travel, **渴望自由** = yearn for freedom.
Near-synonym: **希望** (xīwàng) = to hope (less intense, more general).
cards.englishPhrase: She has a desire to travel.

cards.grammarNote:
**仇恨** (chóuhèn) = hatred / deep hatred (noun). **仇** = enmity/grudge, **恨** = to hate/resent.
**恨** contains the heart radical **忄** (竖心旁) on the left — hatred lives in the heart.
**仇恨** is stronger and more formal than **恨** alone, often used for hatred between groups or for ideological/historical grievance.
**只会** (zhǐ huì) = 'will only / can only': **只会伤害自己** = will only hurt yourself.
**自己** (zìjǐ) = oneself / yourself — a reflexive pronoun that works for all persons: **伤害自己** (hurt oneself).

**快乐** (kuàilè) = happiness / pleasure / joy (noun or adjective). **快** = fast/pleased (contains heart radical **忄** on the left), **乐** = music/joy.
Three near-synonyms for happy — distinguish carefully:
• **快乐** (kuàilè) = general happiness, joy — used for deeper contentment or well-wishes (生日快乐! Happy birthday!)
• **高兴** (gāoxìng) = happy/pleased in the moment — **高** (high) + **兴** (spirit) → high spirits
• **开心** (kāixīn) = cheerful, light-hearted happy — **开** (open) + **心** (heart) → open heart
In the phrase **很高兴认识你**: **高兴** + verb is a polite fixed expression for meeting people.
cards.englishPhrase: It's a pleasure to meet you.

cards.grammarNote:
**痛苦** (tòngkǔ) = pain / suffering / anguish (can be physical or emotional). **痛** = pain/ache, **苦** = bitter/suffering.
Both characters are evocative: 痛 conveys acute pain, 苦 conveys prolonged bitterness or hardship.
Compare: **疼** (téng) = physical pain/ache (my knee hurts = 我膝盖疼), **难受** (nánshòu) = feel bad/uncomfortable (milder).
**痛苦** is often used for deeper emotional or physical suffering: **内心痛苦** (inner anguish), **痛苦的经历** (painful experience).
**非常** (fēicháng) = extremely / very much. Stronger than **很** (very).
cards.englishPhrase: She is in a lot of pain.

cards.grammarNote:
**精力** (jīnglì) = energy / vitality (especially mental/physical vigor). **精** = refined/essence, **力** = strength/power.
Related: **精力充沛** (jīnglì chōngpèi) = full of energy (a common set phrase), **有精力** (yǒu jīnglì) = have energy.
**没什么** (méi shénme) = 'not much / nothing much': **没什么精力** = not much energy. **什么** here softens the negation rather than asking 'what?'
Compare **精力** (mental/physical vitality) vs **能量** (néngliàng = energy in a scientific/physical sense, or informal energy).
cards.englishPhrase: I don't have much energy today.

cards.grammarNote:
**宽慰** (kuānwèi) = relief / comfort / consolation. **宽** = broad/wide/relaxed, **慰** = to comfort/console.
**慰** contains the heart radical **心** at the bottom — comfort goes to the heart.
Related word: **如释重负** (rú shì zhòng fù) = a set expression meaning 'as if a heavy burden has been lifted' — a vivid description of relief.
Near-synonyms: **放心** (fàngxīn) = to feel relieved / feel at ease (literally 'release the heart'), **松了一口气** (sōng le yī kǒu qì) = breathed a sigh of relief.
**感到** (gǎndào) + adjective = 'feel [adjective]': **感到宽慰** = feel relieved.
cards.englishPhrase: I felt great relief after the test.
cards.phrase: 考试后我感到非常宽慰。
cards.phraseRomanized: kǎoshì hòu wǒ gǎndào fēicháng kuānwèi.

cards.grammarNote:
**安慰** (ānwèi) = comfort / to comfort / consolation. Works as both verb and noun.
**安** = peaceful/safe, **慰** = to console (contains **心** heart at bottom).
As a verb: **安慰她** = to comfort her. As a noun: **需要安慰** = need comfort.
**听到** (tīngdào) = 'to hear (and receive)' — **到** is a resultative complement showing the action reached its goal. Compare **听** (trying to hear) vs **听到** (actually heard it).
**消息** (xiāoxi) = news / information. **坏消息** = bad news, **好消息** = good news.
cards.englishPhrase: She needed comfort after the bad news.
cards.phrase: 听到坏消息后她需要安慰。
cards.phraseRomanized: tīngdào huài xiāoxi hòu tā xūyào ānwèi.

cards.grammarNote:
**恨** (hèn) = to hate / resent (verb). Contains the heart radical **忄** (竖心旁) on the left — hatred is felt in the heart.
Compare: **恨** (personal, emotional hatred) vs **仇恨** (chóuhèn) = deep hatred/enmity (more intense, often between groups).
**被** (bèi) marks the passive voice: **被人欺骗** = 'be deceived by people'. Structure: Subject + **被** + (agent) + verb.
The **被** construction often carries a negative or unwanted tone — something unpleasant happened to the subject.
**欺骗** (qīpiàn) = to deceive/cheat.

**喜欢** (xǐhuān) = to like / enjoy. One of the most common verbs in Chinese.
**喜** = joy/delight (contains **口** mouth + **壴** a drum-like element, suggesting joyful noise), **欢** = happy/joyful.
Difference from near-synonyms:
• **喜欢** (xǐhuān) = to like (general preferences, people, things, activities)
• **爱** (ài) = to love (stronger, deeper affection)
• **爱好** (àihào) = hobby / something you love to do (noun)
**喜欢** + noun: 我喜欢这个地方 (I like this place)
**喜欢** + verb phrase: 我喜欢吃辣的 (I like eating spicy food)
Does NOT need a 是: never say ~~我是喜欢~~.

**享受** (xiǎngshòu) = to enjoy / to savor. **享** = to receive/enjoy (as a privilege), **受** = to receive/accept.
More deliberate and savoring than **喜欢** — implies actively enjoying and appreciating something.
**与** (yǔ) = with (more formal/written than **跟** gēn or **和** hé).
**共度** (gòngdù) = to spend (time) together — **共** = together, **度** = to pass/spend (time).
**时光** (shíguāng) = time / the flow of time (more poetic than **时间** shíjiān which is clock time).
cards.englishPhrase: I enjoy spending time with friends.
cards.phrase: 我享受与朋友共度时光。
cards.phraseRomanized: wǒ xiǎngshòu yǔ péngyou gòngdù shíguāng.

cards.grammarNote:
**害怕** (hàipà) = to be afraid of / to fear. **害** = harm/injury, **怕** = to fear.
**怕** contains the heart radical **忄** (竖心旁) on the left — fear is in the heart.
Structure: **我害怕 + [thing/situation]**: 我害怕黑暗 (I'm afraid of the dark), 我害怕蛇 (I'm afraid of snakes).
Note: 害怕 is a verb, NOT an adjective — say **我害怕** not ~~我是害怕~~.
Near-synonym: **恐惧** (kǒngjù) = fear (noun, more literary/formal).
**吃惊** (chī jīng) = to be startled/shocked — different nuance from 害怕.

**使** (shǐ) = to cause / make (something become a certain way). Structure: **使 + object + adjective/verb**.
**使你心情平静** = 'cause your mood to become calm.'
This **使** causative is common with emotions: **使人快乐** (make people happy), **使他愤怒** (make him angry).
Alternative causative: **让** (ràng) is more colloquial: **让你平静** ≈ **使你平静**.
**心情** (xīnqíng) = mood. **心情平静** = 'mood [is] calm' — no verb needed between noun and adjective in Chinese.
cards.phraseRomanized: yīnyuè néng shǐ nǐ xīnqíng píngjìng.

cards.grammarNote:
**使兴奋** = to excite / make (someone) excited. Structure: **使 + person + 兴奋**.
**兴奋** (xīngfèn) = excited/excitement — **兴** = spirit/enthusiasm, **奋** = to exert oneself energetically.
This sentence uses **使大家都很兴奋** — note **都** (all) reinforces that everyone is included.
Alternative colloquial form: **让大家都很兴奋** (ràng — more everyday speech).
Also natural: **大家都很兴奋** (Everyone is very excited) — no causative verb needed if context is clear.
cards.phrase: 这个消息使大家都很兴奋。
cards.phraseRomanized: zhège xiāoxi shǐ dàjiā dōu hěn xīngfèn.

cards.grammarNote:
**激动** (jīdòng) = thrilled / moved / emotionally stirred. **激** = to stir up (water radical 氵), **动** = to move.
Literally: to stir up motion — a vivid description of being emotionally moved.
**激动** is often used for a stronger emotional surge than **兴奋** (excited): 激动得哭了 (so moved I cried), 激动人心 (thrilling/heart-stirring).
**进球** (jìnqiú) = goal (in sports, literally 'enter ball'). **观众** (guānzhòng) = audience/spectators.
cards.phrase: 这个进球使观众非常激动。
cards.phraseRomanized: zhège jìnqiú shǐ guānzhòng fēicháng jīdòng.

cards.grammarNote:
**惹恼** (rěnǎo) = to annoy / irritate / provoke to anger. **惹** = to provoke/stir up, **恼** = annoyed/resentful.
**恼** contains the heart radical **忄** (竖心旁) on the left — annoyance sits in the heart.
Note the **了** after **惹恼**: marks that the annoyance has been completed/achieved — 'did (successfully) annoy.'
Related: **烦** (fán) = to feel annoyed/troubled (milder), **烦人** (fán rén) = annoying (person or thing), **让人烦** = makes one annoyed.
**噪音** (zàoyīn) = noise (unwanted sound) — **噪** = noisy, **音** = sound.

**尴尬** (gāngà) = embarrassed / awkward. One of the most useful emotional adjectives in colloquial Chinese.
Both characters are phonetic-semantic — the 尢 (lame/weak) radical in each hints at an uncomfortable, unsteady feeling.
**感到尴尬** = to feel embarrassed. **感到** (gǎndào) = to feel (followed by an emotion).
Note: **尴尬** describes situations as well as people: **这很尴尬** (This is awkward), **尴尬的沉默** (an awkward silence).
Alternative: **难为情** (nán wéi qíng) = feel embarrassed/shy (a bit more old-fashioned).

**嫉妒** (jídù) = jealousy / to be jealous / to envy. Works as both noun and verb.
**嫉** = to be jealous of (contains 女 woman radical — historical encoding), **妒** = to envy (also contains 女 radical).
Both characters contain the **女** (woman/female) radical. This is a historical artifact and does not carry gender implications today.
**不要嫉妒** = Don't be jealous. **不要** (bùyào) is a standard way to say 'don't' (imperative): **不要哭** (Don't cry), **不要担心** (Don't worry).
Near-synonym: **羡慕** (xiànmù) = admiring envy (wanting what someone has, without the negative spite of 嫉妒).

**激怒** (jīnù) = to infuriate / enrage. Stronger than **惹恼** (annoy).
**激** = to provoke/stir up, **怒** = rage/fury (contains **心** heart at bottom — rage erupts from the heart).
**行为** (xíngwéi) = behavior / conduct. **他的行为** = his behavior.
**了** after **激怒**: resultative — the infuriation was achieved/completed.
Note the causative structure: **[cause] + 激怒 + 了 + [person]** = '[cause] infuriated [person]'.
cards.phraseRomanized: tā de xíngwéi jīnù le tā.

cards.grammarNote:
**使愤怒** = to anger someone / make someone furious. Structure: **使 + person + 愤怒**.
**愤怒** (fènnù) = anger/fury — **愤** = indignation (contains **心** heart below), **怒** = rage (also contains **心** heart below).
Both characters have the heart radical **心** at the bottom — intense anger comes from deep within.
**不公平** (bù gōngpíng) = unfair/unjust. **公平** = fair, **不公平** = unfair (adding **不** negates).
**待遇** (dàiyù) = treatment / conditions. **而** (ér) = 'and therefore' / 'as a result' — connects cause and effect in a formal way.
cards.phrase: 不公平的待遇使他愤怒。
cards.phraseRomanized: bù gōngpíng de dàiyù shǐ tā fènnù.

cards.grammarNote:
**高兴** (gāoxìng) = happy / glad / pleased. **高** = high/tall, **兴** = spirit/enthusiasm.
Literal: high spirits → happy. One of the most common words for happy in Chinese.
Three near-synonyms for happy:
• **高兴** (gāoxìng) — pleased/happy in the moment: **我很高兴认识你** (Pleased to meet you)
• **开心** (kāixīn) — cheerful, open-hearted: **开** (open) + **心** (heart)
• **快乐** (kuàilè) — deep joy/happiness: used for birthdays, general contentment
**使** (shǐ) + person + **高兴** = 'make someone happy'. Note: **这份礼物** = 'this gift' — **份** is the measure word for gifts, portions.
cards.phraseRomanized: zhè fèn lǐwù shǐ tā hěn gāoxìng.

cards.grammarNote:
**微笑** (wēixiào) = smile (noun or verb). **微** = tiny/slight, **笑** = to laugh/smile.
Literal: a slight laugh → a smile. Compare **笑** alone = to laugh or smile (broader), **大笑** (dà xiào) = to laugh loudly.
**对** (duì) before a person = 'toward / to': **对我微笑** = 'smiled at me.' **对** marks the direction or target of an action.
Near-synonym: **笑了笑** (xiàole xiào) = smiled briefly — verb reduplication for a quick, light action.

**笑** (xiào) = to laugh / to smile (general). The character depicts a person bending with joy — a person leaning forward laughing.
Variants: **大笑** (dàxiào) = laugh loudly, **苦笑** (kǔxiào) = bitter smile, **微笑** (wēixiào) = gentle smile, **嘲笑** (cháoxiào) = to mock/ridicule (laugh at).
**一起** (yīqǐ) = together. Placed before the verb: **一起笑** = laughed together.
**了** after the verb: marks the completed event — they laughed (it happened).

**哭** (kū) = to cry / weep. The character shows a person (人) with tears — a pictographic origin.
**开始** (kāishǐ) + verb = 'to begin to [verb]': **开始哭** = began to cry, **开始笑** = began to laugh.
**了** here at the end: marks the new situation — the child has now begun crying (a change).
Related expressions: **哭出来** (kū chūlái) = burst into tears, **哭着** (kūzhe) = while crying, **哭得很厉害** (crying really hard — degree complement).

**叹气** (tànqì) = to sigh. **叹** = to sigh/lament, **气** = breath/air/energy.
Literal: to exhale breath in lament → to sigh. **叹** contains the **口** (mouth) radical — the sound comes from the mouth.
**深深地叹了口气** = 'deeply sighed a breath.' The measure **口气** (kǒu qì) = 'a breath' is the natural object of **叹**: **叹了一口气** = sighed once.
**深深地** = deeply (adverb: adjective reduplicated + **地**). Reduplication of adjectives intensifies: 深→深深, 慢→慢慢.
cards.phraseRomanized: tā shēnshēn de tàn le kǒu qì.

cards.grammarNote:
**皱眉** (zhòuméi) = to frown / knit one's brows. **皱** = to wrinkle/crease, **眉** = eyebrow.
The fuller form in the phrase: **皱起了眉头** = 'wrinkled up the brow.' **眉头** (méitóu) = the brow/forehead area between the eyebrows.
**起** as a directional complement suggests an upward or emerging motion: **皱起** = wrinkle up.
**对** + question/topic = 'regarding / in response to': **对这个问题皱眉** = frowned at this question.
cards.phrase: 她对这个问题皱起了眉头。
cards.phraseRomanized: tā duì zhège wèntí zhòu qǐ le méitóu.

cards.grammarNote:
**尖叫** (jiānjiào) = to scream / shriek. **尖** = sharp/pointed, **叫** = to call out/shout.
Literal: a sharp cry → a scream. **叫** contains the **口** (mouth) radical — it's about the voice.
**起来** (qǐlái) as a complement after a verb indicates that an action begins or bursts out: **尖叫起来** = burst into screaming, **哭起来** = burst into tears, **笑起来** = burst out laughing.
**时** (shí) = when/at the time (more formal than **的时候**): **看到蜘蛛时** = when she saw the spider.
cards.englishPhrase: She screamed when she saw the spider.
cards.phrase: 她看到蜘蛛时尖叫起来。
cards.phraseRomanized: tā kàndào zhīzhū shí jiānjiào qǐlái.

cards.grammarNote:
**大喊** (dà hǎn) = to shout / yell loudly. **大** = big/loud, **喊** = to call out/shout.
**大** intensifies the action: **大喊** (shout loudly), **大哭** (cry loudly), **大笑** (laugh loudly), **大叫** (yell). This **大** pattern is very productive.
**喊** contains the **口** (mouth) radical — physical voice-based actions often have this radical.
**不要对我大喊** — note **对** again: 'toward me' / 'at me.' **对** marks the direction of verbal or physical action.
cards.phraseRomanized: bù yào duì wǒ dà hǎn.

cards.grammarNote:
**放松** (fàngsōng) = to relax / loosen up. **放** = to release/let go, **松** = loose/slack.
Literal: to let go and loosen → to relax. A very natural image of tension releasing.
**一下** (yīxià) after a verb softens it and suggests doing the action briefly or just a little: **放松一下** = relax a bit.
**试着** (shìzhe) = 'try to' (in the sense of making an attempt): **试着放松** = try to relax. Compare **试试** (shìshi) = give it a try (verb reduplication).
Related: **放松心情** = relax one's mood, **轻松** (qīngsōng) = light and relaxed (adjective).

Adverb form: **生气地** / **愤怒地** (shēngqì de / fènnù de — angrily).
Add **地** after the adjective: 他生气地走了。(He left angrily.)

**发怒** (fānù) = to fly into a rage / lose one's temper. **发** = to emit/break out (as in 发火 = to flare up), **怒** = fury/rage (contains **心** heart at bottom).
**因...而...** (yīn...ér...) = because of...therefore... (formal pattern): **因压力而发怒** = got angry because of stress.
Compare angry words: **生气** (shēngqì) = everyday annoyed/angry, **发怒** (fānù) = erupt in rage, **愤怒** (fènnù) = deep anger/fury (noun or state), **发火** (fāhuǒ) = blow one's top (colloquial).
cards.phraseRomanized: tā yīn yālì ér fānù le.

cards.grammarNote:
**害怕** (hàipà) = afraid / to be afraid of. **害** = harm/injury, **怕** = to fear (contains **忄** heart radical on left).
As a stative verb: **你害怕狗吗？** = Are you afraid of dogs? Note: Chinese emotion words are often verbs — say **我害怕** (I am afraid), NOT ~~我是害怕~~.
**吗** (ma) at the end of a statement turns it into a yes/no question. Simple and essential — the sentence structure does not change.
Near-synonym: **恐惧** (kǒngjù) = fear (more formal noun), **担心** (dānxīn) = worried (about something specific).

**受惊** (shòujīng) = to be startled / frightened (by a sudden shock). **受** = to receive/experience, **惊** = a fright/start.
**惊** contains the **忄** heart radical — a fright is felt in the heart.
Compare: **害怕** (hàipà) = ongoing fear/being afraid of something; **受惊** = startled/frightened by a sudden event (more acute and momentary).
**看起来** (kàn qǐlái) = 'looks like / appears' — a very useful phrase: **看起来很疲惫** (looks very tired), **看起来不开心** (looks unhappy).

Adverb form: **焦虑地** (jiāolǜ de — anxiously).
Add **地** after the adjective: 她焦虑地等待着。(She waited anxiously.)
cards.englishPhrase: She felt anxious before the exam.
cards.phraseRomanized: kǎoshì qián tā gǎndào hěn jiāolǜ.

cards.grammarNote:
Adverb form: **紧张地** (jǐnzhāng de — nervously).
Add **地** after the adjective: 他紧张地说话。(He spoke nervously.)

Adverb form: **兴奋地** (xīngfèn de — excitedly).
Add **地** after the adjective: 她兴奋地叫了起来。(She cried out excitedly.)
cards.englishPhrase: The children are excited about the trip.
cards.phrase: 孩子们对这次旅行很兴奋。
cards.phraseRomanized: háizimen duì zhè cì lǚxíng hěn xīngfèn.

cards.grammarNote:
**欢喜** (huānxǐ) = joyful / delighted. **欢** = joyful/enthusiastic, **喜** = joy/delight.
Both characters relate to happiness — together they form a more emphatic expression of delight.
**欢喜** is slightly more literary and warm than **高兴** or **开心**. Often seen in formal blessings and traditional contexts.
**整天** (zhěngtiān) = all day long / the whole day. **整** = whole/entire: **整天**, **整周** (whole week), **整年** (whole year).
**都** (dōu) = all — here emphasizing the continuity: she was joyful ALL day.

**满足** (mǎnzú) = content / satisfied / fulfilled. **满** = full/filled, **足** = sufficient/enough (also means 'foot/leg').
Literal: full and sufficient → satisfied/content.
**满足** + **感** (gǎn) = **满足感** = sense of satisfaction / fulfillment.
**对...很满足** = 'satisfied with...': **对自己的生活很满足** = content with one's own life.
Note the structure: **他对自己的生活很满足** — **对** introduces what the satisfaction is about. This **对** + topic pattern is very common with emotions.
cards.englishPhrase: He is content with his life.
cards.phrase: 他对自己的生活很满足。
cards.phraseRomanized: tā duì zìjǐ de shēnghuó hěn mǎnzú.

cards.grammarNote:
**有压力** (yǒu yālì) = to have/feel stress (literally 'have pressure'). **有** + noun describes states very naturally in Chinese.
**压力** (yālì) = pressure/stress: **压** = to press down, **力** = strength/force.
The phrase structure: **感到 + 压力 + 很大** = 'feel that the pressure is very big' → feel very stressed.
Note: **大** and **小** are used for intensity of abstract nouns: **压力大** (a lot of stress), **压力小** (little stress). Chinese often avoids 'much/little' in favor of 'big/small' for abstract quantities.
cards.englishPhrase: I feel very stressed this week.
cards.phraseRomanized: zhè zhōu wǒ gǎndào yālì hěn dà.

cards.grammarNote:
**不堪重负** (bùkān zhòngfù) = overwhelmed / unable to bear a heavy burden. A four-character set expression (成语-like).
**不堪** = unable to bear / can't stand, **重负** = heavy burden (**重** = heavy, **负** = burden/load).
**被** (bèi) passive construction: **她被工作量压得不堪重负** = 'She was pressed by the workload to the point of being overwhelmed.'
Structure: Subject + **被** + agent + verb + **得** + degree/result. The **得** here introduces the result of the action.
**工作量** (gōngzuòliàng) = workload (**量** = quantity/amount).
cards.englishPhrase: She felt overwhelmed by the workload.
cards.phrase: 她被工作量压得不堪重负。
cards.phraseRomanized: tā bèi gōngzuòliàng yā de bùkān zhòngfù.

cards.grammarNote:
**精力充沛** (jīnglì chōngpèi) = full of energy / energetic. A very common set phrase.
**精力** = vitality/energy, **充沛** = overflowing/abundant (**充** = fill up, **沛** = abundant/flourishing).
Used as a predicate: **他精力充沛** (He is full of energy). Also as a modifier: **精力充沛的人** (an energetic person).
**醒来** (xǐng lái) = to wake up (and come to). **来** as a directional complement suggests coming into a state — returning to consciousness.
**感到** + adjective: **感到精力充沛** = felt energetic.
cards.phraseRomanized: tā xǐng lái gǎndào jīnglì chōngpèi.

cards.grammarNote:
Adverb form: **疲惫地** (píbèi de — wearily).
Add **地** after the adjective: 他疲惫地拖着脚步。(He dragged his feet wearily.)

**筋疲力尽** (jīnpí lìjìn) = completely exhausted. A four-character set expression (成语).
**筋疲** = muscles exhausted (**筋** = tendons/muscles, **疲** = tired), **力尽** = strength all used up (**力** = strength, **尽** = exhausted/used up).
Every part of this phrase emphasizes total depletion — muscles tired, strength gone.
Compare: **累** (lèi) = tired (everyday), **疲惫** (píbèi) = exhausted (more formal), **筋疲力尽** = utterly drained (vivid set phrase).
**比赛结束后** = after the race finished — **结束** (jiéshù) = to end/finish.
cards.englishPhrase: After the race, he was exhausted.
cards.phrase: 比赛结束后，他筋疲力尽。
cards.phraseRomanized: bǐsài jiéshù hòu, tā jīnpí lìjìn.

cards.grammarNote:
**好奇** (hàoqí) = curious. **好** (hào, 4th tone here) = to like/be fond of, **奇** = strange/unusual.
Note the tone: **好** in 好奇 is 4th tone (hào = to be fond of), NOT 3rd tone (hǎo = good). **好奇** = 'fond of the strange' → curious.
Other **好**-as-a-verb compounds: **好学** (hàoxué) = eager to learn, **好胜** (hàoshèng) = competitive.
**对...很好奇** = 'very curious about...': **对一切都很好奇** = curious about everything.
**一切** (yīqiè) = everything, always paired with **都** (all).
cards.phraseRomanized: tā duì yīqiè dōu hěn hàoqí.
